top_categories.workout_context: 300 miles. Your legs will beg to quit after 120. This trains the power output you need from mile 130 onward.
Race-pace efforts that mimic Cycle Oregon — Rally's demands. Pacing, fueling, and tactical decisions under fatigue. Practice the race before August.
300 miles rewards fat oxidation. This volume work shifts your fuel mix so you're still burning fat at hour 5, not bonking at hour 3.
Every hard session works better with a bigger aerobic base. At 300 miles, base fitness is the difference between racing and surviving.
Endurance with intensity spikes baked in. Exactly what 17,000ft of climbing on paved Oregon back roads with optional gravel does to your power file on race day.
Cycle Oregon — Rally's paved Oregon back roads with optional gravel forces constant power changes. Surge over the rough stuff, settle on the smooth, repeat for 300 miles.
17,000ft of climbing means repeated surges above threshold. VO2max work is how you survive the fifth climb, not just the first.
57ft/mi means long efforts at or near FTP on every climb. If your threshold cracks at minute 15, you'll walk the rest.
Tempo is Cycle Oregon — Rally's race pace. The wattage you'll hold for 300 miles on paved Oregon back roads with optional gravel. Train it until it's automatic.
At Cycle Oregon — Rally, surges push you above threshold and you have to recover while still riding. Over-unders train exactly that.
88–92% FTP is where you'll spend the hardest sustained miles of Cycle Oregon — Rally. Train it until it feels boring.
17,000ft of climbing on grades that change every minute. You need to switch from seated to standing without losing rhythm.

race_overlay.nutrition: Ultra-distance fueling for 300 miles: 80–100g carbs/hour from mile 1 — don’t wait until you’re hungry. Practice your exact race-day nutrition in every long training ride. Carry backup calories. 300 miles burns 8,000–12,000+ calories — you cannot replace them all, but you must try.

pack_summary: This 10-workout pack focuses on Durability, Race Simulation, and HVLI Extended to prepare you for 300 miles of paved oregon back roads with optional gravel in Southern Oregon — Grants Pass and Jacksonville basecamps (2026). Route and region change annually..
